item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t think it’s a balrog. If I were down in a burning coal mine fleeing for my life from a giant horned fire monster I wouldn’t stick around long enough to get a good look at it, especially since most of its body is probably obscured by smoke anyway. It’d be easy to confused among all the smoke and fire given off by the creature itself and the burning coal around it, so it’s no surprise that reports vary as to its appearance. A LotR fan is already going to expect a balrog when encountering a fire demon in an abandoned mine, so they’ll be more likely to mistake random shapes in the fire and smoke for wings and the iconic weapons. It’s just human pattern recognition at work.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The first and fourth images that are used in this article are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and are sourced as follows:

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public Domain
Title: Censign.jpg
Author: JohnDS
Release year: 2006

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 2.5
Title: Centralia smoke rising.jpg
Author: Jrmski
Release year: 2006

The second and third images that were used in this article have been replaced with the following images, which are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public Domain
Title: Pdr 1647.jpg
Author: JohnDS
Release year: 2006

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY 2.0
Title: Centralia, PA
Author: Travis Goodspeed
Release year: 2009

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could obviously be underneath Silent Hill, Pennsylvania. Maybe we could mention the cognito hazard above ground, as an addendum or a second independent SCP entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the initial description: "ha! sounds like a balrog! I wonder if that was intentional? Probably not. Moving on…"

when it got to the whether-it-has-wings debate: "Ok, it's DEFINITELY intentional that it's a Balrog."

Reading the comments: "if you didn't mean for it to be a balrog, why the whether-it-has-wings bit??"

In general: I thought that the Centralia fires being caused by a balrog Nordic fire demon was an interesting premise. The way it kept going after that — adding more and more connections between the SCP and Centraila news — made me think it was building up to something, and I was looking forward to see where it went. Alas, it wasn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the execution of a Centralian entity could be done in a much more creative way than an inconsistent series of pop culture quips. I'm not particularly one to talk, as I have my own issues writing, but this whole SCP just leaves me more irritated than interested.

Other than just reinstating an alternate history in which the Centralia coal fire is generic demon entity-1179 just seems a bit lame compared to the history of the region and the many inspirations that come out of the town itself. I'm not saying it should be a Silent Hill SCP or anything like that. But I think at the very least, you could drop the weapons and incredibly inconsistent forms. Make it far more mysterious, more Keter. At this point, it's just someone else's work with SCP stapled onto it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You shall not pass!
Just kidding. I know it is Surtur, but described in text it sounds exactly like Durin's Bane.
+1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if Centralia was caused by a Balrog?
And what if the Balrog was made of Bees?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, everyone's going on about balrogs, but I just love the story of the Foundation having to politik in order to "contain" a giant monster that they don't understand, as they weren't so vigorous back in ye-olden-days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but think that the actual reason for the Centralia Mine Fire is quite a lot more interesting and thematically resonant than 'a fire demon did it'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are both based on the same piece of mythology. Don't get me wrong, Tolkien was a wonderful writer, but his universe is the result of a careful study of various existing European lores.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for necroposting, but I would like to notice, that "Balrog" isn't a name of singular entity. Balrogs are spirits of fire corrupted by Morgoth (one of non-corrupted fire spirit, Arien, became Sun itself). There are named Balrogs, for example Gothmog, Lord of Balrogs. So "Balrog" is closer to "elf" or "wizard" than to "Legolas" and "Gandalf".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is it that everyone is talking about it being balrog here? I'm a true marvel fans, and I say say he is Surtur.

Hell, he is in marvel comics. Someone pass me my hammer?

Fortunately, I am mighty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this technically is merely based on the same things as balrogs were, but the fact is that at very few points does this strike me as more than just "the Centralia fires? A balrog did them". Either of these elements could be fleshed out so much more; here they work against each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand that its not a balrog, but as long as you say it has a sword and whip of fire that will be the overwhelming opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant!

Taking a real-life event, and making it seem as though it is indeed something more then what you would want it to be.

Grand work, certainly one of the more unique SCPs I've seen around here (in terms of background-story).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Real life oddity meets Foundation lore.

Honestly the whole fire giant thing could be toned down redacted leaving more to the imagination.

Just the fact of how the foundation successfully Keter contained it in a way that fits real life and the way the foundation appears to do things gets this a +1 up vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote, i like seeing the blend of modern history with foundation operations. I didn't think Balrog at all while reading it, but then again I'm not a LOTR nerd, unlike most of your other readers. Plus, Pennsylvania is my homestate so, PA HYPE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not clinical enough. Like, Balrog/Norse fire demon debate entirely aside, the tone in this is not up to par on clinicality. Neutral voting because I'm sleepy and don't trust my own judgment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I love this. Who cares if it sounds like a Balrog or not. It's great either way. I love Centralia and have always wanted to check it out since I only live an hour and a half away. This has got to be one of my favorite SCPs now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Scrubs, people are completely allowed to have opinions you disagree with. That's what makes this a critique site instead of a hivemind. It's okay to express your disagreement, but here you are coming off as a bit interrogative.
Anthropomorphism technically is the exact same thing as personification, but in common usage it functions as a more extensive form of it. Personification is the attribution of a few human traits to an object: "The cat waltzed across the table in search of food."
Anthropomorphism, as it is general held, is making that object almost completely human-like: "The cat talks, wears a suit, and has a job in a call center. On Fridays, it takes waltzing lessons."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: anthromorphism? As in personification? I'm sorry i don't understand what you mean, how could you be against a literary device. like Jack Frost or Zeus?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was tempted to downvote due to my being anti-anthromorphism, but it had just enough random "weird-shit-o-meter" entries to amuse me. But then the comments going LOTR Balrog threaten to ruin it again for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted. It's just another "real life SCP", and… fire demon? Really? Another bunch of such objects - and we will know, that there are no such thing as disasters. It's just cover-ups of Great, Powerful And Almighty Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [ignore this post please]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic Balrog you have here. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote to this comment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
like others have said, drop the sword and whip and itll look great.

other than that though i really enjoy this one mailnly for the lack of expungents and ties to reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how it's taken real life history and turned it into a SCP. While the whip and sword might be a bit much for me, it isn't enough for me to dislike it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is it bad that it's a Balrog?
Most of what we think of as classic high-fantasy elements are Tolkien extracted, he invented the plural of "elf" for crying out loud. If you just dismiss a concept because it is too similar to LotR then you have to rule out fantasy-inspired SCPs nearly as a class of objects.

Just my opinion, but I like this Balrog. Anything less…i dunno, "epic" isn't the word I want…wouldn't be able to stack up against the impressiveness of the irl fires.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not downvoting, and I get that there is a reason that it has a whip and a sword, but for some reason the fact that it has a whip and a sword is killing it for me.

Look, man. Just take out the whip and the reference to the wings/no wings debate, and there won't be any question about it being the Balrog. Even if that's not what you intended, that's what people are getting out of the article. That's not their fault for being poor readers, but your fault of not sufficiently conveying that it ain't a Balrog.

Seconding all of this. There's no reason it has to have the whip or a sword (or for you to specifically namecheck the Great Balrog Wings Debate), and those elements hurt the piece because they seem like cheesy pop culture references. Is that fair? No. Is it true anyway? Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look, man. Just take out the whip and the reference to the wings/no wings debate, and there won't be any question about it being the Balrog. Even if that's not what you intended, that's what people are getting out of the article. That's not their fault for being poor readers, but your fault of not sufficiently conveying that it ain't a Balrog.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoting. It's a balrog, no matter what the author claims.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not downvoting, and I get that there is a reason that it has a whip and a sword, but for some reason the fact that it has a whip and a sword is killing it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technically, it's not a Balrog any more than any random elven archer is Legolas, any long-bearded wizard is Gandalf, or any underground Dwarven ruin are the Mines of Moria. Hell, the author himself said that he based it off the old legend that basically WAS the inspiration for the Balrog.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I'm not saying it's a Balrog…but it's a Balrog.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my initial thought as well, but then seeing it connected to the Burning Mines in Centralia I thought "well maybe not." Frankly I enjoy it, but I can see why the LotR connection probably won't go away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Not sure about this one. The general idea is great, but unfortunately Lord of the Rings is entirely too popular, and a lot of people will read this as "it's a Balrog". I know you based the creature on a fire giant, but most people aren't going to know that. I'd really recommend dropping the whip and the sword from the description. Additionally, I like the idea that people simply cannot agree on what it looks like now it's no longer dormant, and having a wide range of descriptions - some of which are clearly something other than a Balrog - might help.

Other than that - and the line "become invisible and without form, appearing as a giant flying shade", which doesn't sit well with me for some reason - I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really clever. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't you mean how troll you are?

It's a pun based around the fact that it was inspired by Viking mythology, using a different Norse mythological creature. No, I am not calling you a troll. Yes, this is a disclaimer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had planned to do a Centralia SCP one of these days. This is well-done.

(See what I did there? A play on words. How droll I am.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anyone find my hammer lying around?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did look at a bunch of different mythological fire demons, ultimately choosing to go with the Norse jotun Surtr as my primary inspiration. I did see Balrogs in my research, but decided I didn't just want to transplant a beastie from a relatively modern and VERY popular mythos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking the same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this:

Eyewitnesses cannot agree whether or not it has wings.

isn't a reference to the LotR fandom's eternal argument about whether or not Balrogs have wings?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've gone through and tried to improve both the formatting and wording. If people have specific bits they think I need to fix, I'd be happy to keep working at it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you're missing the point here - Gandalf is currently fighting an ancient demon in the heart of a coal mine in Pennsylvania.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, as much as I hate IRL back story SCPs.

I'm upvoting, but some of the wording is awkward and the formatting is painful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1179 is not a Balrog, though Tolkien did use Norse mythology as a source for his Balrogs the same as I did. Case of two different authors using the same source material.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Classified footage of Agent G engaging SCP-1179
